title: Red Eye — Approach
oneLiner: Common presentation; must exclude sight-threatening causes (acute glaucoma, orbital cellulitis, endophthalmitis, herpetic keratitis) before treating conjunctivitis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute angle closure glaucoma** – The eye is rock-hard (IOP >40-50 mmHg). Single finding: **fixed, mid-dilated pupil + cloudy cornea + severe pain + nausea**. Vision lost irreversibly within hours.
- **Orbital cellulitis** – **Proptosis, restricted eye movements, fever, + relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD)**. Can progress to cavernous sinus thrombosis and meningitis.
- **Endophthalmitis** – **Hypopyon (pus in anterior chamber) + severe pain + blurred vision + recent intraocular surgery/injection**. Emergency vitreous tap needed.
- **Herpes simplex keratitis** – **Dendritic ulcer on fluorescein staining + significantly reduced corneal sensation**. Steroids worsen it catastrophically.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Redness (diffuse or sectoral)
  - Pain (ache, sharp, photophobia)
  - Blurred vision (transient or persistent)
  - Discharge (watery, mucoid, purulent)
  - Foreign body sensation, photophobia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Visual acuity** – first thing (any reduction = red flag)
  - **Conjunctival injection** – diffuse (conjunctivitis) vs ciliary flush (iritis/keratitis)
  - **Corneal clarity** – cloudy = keratitis, glaucoma, or corneal oedema
  - **Pupil shape & reaction** – small/irregular (iritis), mid-dilated fixed (glaucoma), normal (conjunctivitis)
  - **Fluorescein staining** – dendritic ulcer (HSV), abrasion, ulcer
  - **Intraocular pressure (IOP)** – digital vs tonometry; high = glaucoma
  - **Anterior chamber** – cells/flare = iritis (slit lamp needed)
  - **Extraocular movements + proptosis** – orbital cellulitis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Viral conjunctivitis** – watery discharge, preauricular node, recent URTI; no photophobia, normal acuity
  - **Bacterial conjunctivitis** – purulent discharge, sticky eyelids; normal cornea, no photophobia
  - **Allergic conjunctivitis** – itching, bilateral, stringy discharge, papillae
  - **Subconjunctival haemorrhage** – well-demarcated blood, no pain, no vision change; usually benign
  - **Keratitis (including HSV)** – pain, photophobia, fluorescein uptake, corneal opacity; reduced vision
  - **Anterior uveitis (iritis)** – ciliary flush, small/poorly reactive pupil, photophobia, cells/flare; normal IOP
  - **Acute angle closure glaucoma** – cloudy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, high IOP, severe pain, nausea
  - **Episcleritis/scleritis** – sectoral injection, mild pain (episcleritis) vs deep boring pain (scleritis); vision normal
  - **Orbital cellulitis** – proptosis, restricted movement, fever, RAPD
  - **Endophthalmitis** – hypopyon, vitreous haze, severe pain, recent surgery
- **Splitter:** *Fluorescein staining* — positive = keratitis > iritis; negative but photophobia + ciliary flush = iritis
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart or pinhole)
  - Slit lamp examination (if available)
  - Fluorescein staining (sterile strip, cobalt blue light)
  - Intraocular pressure (tonometry or digital estimation)
  - Swab for bacterial culture if purulent discharge (for Chlamydia/gonorrhoea if sexually active)
- **Bloods:**
  - Only if suspect orbital cellulitis: FBC, CRP, blood cultures, consider ESR/ANA if uveitis recurrent
  - No routine bloods for pure red eye
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Orbital CT** (with contrast) if orbital cellulitis suspected
  - Second-line: MRI brain/orbits if cavernous sinus thrombosis
  - Not indicated for conjunctivitis, iritis, keratitis, glaucoma
- **Special tests:**
  - Corneal scrape for microbiology (if dendritic ulcer atypical or contact lens keratitis)
  - Anterior chamber paracentesis (only in endophthalmitis under ophthalmology)
- **Never-miss test:** **Fluorescein staining** – omitting this risks missing herpetic keratitis, which would be worsened by steroids
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Acute angle closure glaucoma:**
    - IV acetazolamide 500mg stat (or PO if no IV access)
    - Topical pilocarpine 2% (one drop, may repeat)
    - Topical beta-blocker (e.g. timolol 0.5% one drop)
    - Urgent ophthalmology referral
  - **Orbital cellulitis:**
    - IV ceftriaxone 2g OD + metronidazole 500mg TDS
    - CT orbit + sinuses
    - Ophthalmology + ENT referral
  - **Herpetic keratitis:**
    - Topical aciclovir 3% ointment 5 times daily
    - **NO steroids** (ever until clear by slit lamp)
    - Ophthalmology review within 24h
  - **Endophthalmitis:**
    - Urgent ophthalmology – vitreous tap + intravitreal antibiotics (do not delay in ED)
  - **Simple conjunctivitis:**
    - Chloramphenicol eye drops 0.5% – 1-2 drops q2h for 24h then q4h (first-line bacterial)
    - Fusidic acid eye drops 1% – alternative (especially if staph suspected)
    - If viral – supportive (cool compresses, artificial tears)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If admitted for orbital cellulitis: continue IV antibiotics, monitor vision, EOM, and proptosis hourly
  - If admitted for acute glaucoma (after initial drops): monitor IOP every 4h, prepare for laser iridotomy
  - If herpetic keratitis: review by ophthalmology daily; consider oral aciclovir if severe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Conjunctivitis – complete 7-day course of drops
  - Iritis – topical steroids (dexamethasone 0.1%) under ophthalmology + cycloplegic (e.g. cyclopentolate)
  - Glaucoma – long-term ophthalmology follow-up
  - Keratitis – ophthalmology follow-up for scarring
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** *Only relevant for orbital cellulitis or acute glaucoma*
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline IV at 125mL/h if septic/oral not tolerated (orbital cellulitis)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if not contraindicated)
  - **Diet:** As tolerated (unless NBM for theatre)
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 1-2h initially; local protocol for orbital cellulitis (often hourly neuro-obs including pupil checks)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Chloramphenicol eye drops 0.5%** – 1-2 drops q2h for 24h then q4h for 7 days (bacterial conjunctivitis)
  - **Fusidic acid eye drops 1%** – 1 drop BD for 7 days (alternative)
  - **Aciclovir eye ointment 3%** – 5 times daily for 14 days (herpetic keratitis)
  - **Acetazolamide (acute glaucoma)** – 500mg IV/PO stat
  - **Pilocarpine 2% eye drops** – 1 drop stat, may repeat in 15 min (acute glaucoma)
  - **Timolol 0.5% eye drops** – 1 drop stat (acute glaucoma)
  - **Ceftriaxone** – 2g IV OD (orbital cellulitis)
  - **Metronidazole** – 500mg IV TDS (orbital cellulitis)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Ciprofloxacin 0.3% eye drops** – for contact lens–associated keratitis (pseudomonas)
  - **Prednisolone eye drops 0.5-1%** – for iritis (only under ophthalmology)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - **Topical steroids** → absolute contraindication if herpetic keratitis suspected (until confirmed by slit lamp)
  - **Acetazolamide** → avoid in severe renal/hepatic failure, pregnancy, sulfa allergy
  - **Pilocarpine** → avoid in asthma, bradycardia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Acetazolamide** – reduce dose in renal impairment (CrCl <50: use with caution)
  - **Ceftriaxone** – no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Topical steroids prescribed without fluorescein staining** – can cause devastating corneal damage in herpetic keratitis. **Never prescribe steroid eye drops until HSV excluded.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Vision loss (glaucoma, endophthalmitis, orbital cellulitis)
  - Corneal perforation (severe keratitis)
  - Cavernous sinus thrombosis (orbital cellulitis)
  - Meningitis (orbital cellulitis)
  - Steroid-induced glaucoma (if steroids used inappropriately)
- **Chronic:**
  - Corneal scarring (keratitis)
  - Synechiae (iritis)
  - Chronic glaucoma (angle closure/steroid-induced)
  - Recurrent infection (HSV keratitis)
  - Loss of vision
sections.Prognosis:
- Simple conjunctivitis – excellent, resolves in 5-7 days with treatment.
- Acute angle closure glaucoma – guarded; each hour of IOP >40 reduces chance of vision recovery. Early treatment (<12h) has better outcome.
- Herpetic keratitis – good with prompt antivirals; recurrence common (20-50% within 1 year).
- Orbital cellulitis – good if treated early; delay can cause blindness or death.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check visual acuity and do fluorescein staining** in every red eye – these are the two steps most likely to change your management.
- **In contact lens wearers, assume pseudomonas keratitis** until proven otherwise – refer same day to ophthalmology.
- **If you suspect iritis, put on cycloplegic (cyclopentolate 1%)** to relieve pain and prevent synechiae – but only after excluding glaucoma by IOP and slit lamp.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Prescribing topical steroids without slit lamp examination** – this is the most common serious error. It can turn herpetic keratitis into a corneal perforation.
- **Ignoring photophobia or reduced vision** – these are red flags for keratitis or iritis. Do not dismiss as "just conjunctivitis."
- **Using loperamide or antihistamines for allergic conjunctivitis without examining** – may mask underlying keratitis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening pain, especially if woke from sleep
  - Vision becomes blurry or decreases
  - New photophobia or intense eye ache
  - Contact lens wearer with any new symptom
  - Fever, headache, or double vision
- **Follow-up:**
  - Conjunctivitis – GP in 3-5 days if not settling
  - Allergic – GP or optician
  - Keratitis/iritis/glaucoma – ophthalmology within 24h (or same day if sight-threatening)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ophthalmology registrar** for:
  - Acute angle closure glaucoma (rock-hard eye, fixed pupil, cloudy cornea)
  - Orbital cellulitis (proptosis, fever, EOM restriction, RAPD)
  - Endophthalmitis (hypopyon, recent surgery, severe pain)
  - Herpetic keratitis (dendritic ulcer, reduced corneal sensation)
  - Any red eye with vision loss, severe pain, or suspected sight threat
- **Call ICU/CRASH team** if:
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if orbital cellulitis causing sepsis)
  - GCS <15 (cavernous sinus thrombosis)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or signs of meningitis

rawMarkdown:
# Red Eye — Approach

**One-liner:** Common presentation; must exclude sight-threatening causes (acute glaucoma, orbital cellulitis, endophthalmitis, herpetic keratitis) before treating conjunctivitis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Acute angle closure glaucoma** – The eye is rock-hard (IOP >40-50 mmHg). Single finding: **fixed, mid-dilated pupil + cloudy cornea + severe pain + nausea**. Vision lost irreversibly within hours.
- **Orbital cellulitis** – **Proptosis, restricted eye movements, fever, + relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD)**. Can progress to cavernous sinus thrombosis and meningitis.
- **Endophthalmitis** – **Hypopyon (pus in anterior chamber) + severe pain + blurred vision + recent intraocular surgery/injection**. Emergency vitreous tap needed.
- **Herpes simplex keratitis** – **Dendritic ulcer on fluorescein staining + significantly reduced corneal sensation**. Steroids worsen it catastrophically.

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Redness (diffuse or sectoral)
  - Pain (ache, sharp, photophobia)
  - Blurred vision (transient or persistent)
  - Discharge (watery, mucoid, purulent)
  - Foreign body sensation, photophobia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - **Visual acuity** – first thing (any reduction = red flag)
  - **Conjunctival injection** – diffuse (conjunctivitis) vs ciliary flush (iritis/keratitis)
  - **Corneal clarity** – cloudy = keratitis, glaucoma, or corneal oedema
  - **Pupil shape & reaction** – small/irregular (iritis), mid-dilated fixed (glaucoma), normal (conjunctivitis)
  - **Fluorescein staining** – dendritic ulcer (HSV), abrasion, ulcer
  - **Intraocular pressure (IOP)** – digital vs tonometry; high = glaucoma
  - **Anterior chamber** – cells/flare = iritis (slit lamp needed)
  - **Extraocular movements + proptosis** – orbital cellulitis

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Viral conjunctivitis** – watery discharge, preauricular node, recent URTI; no photophobia, normal acuity
  - **Bacterial conjunctivitis** – purulent discharge, sticky eyelids; normal cornea, no photophobia
  - **Allergic conjunctivitis** – itching, bilateral, stringy discharge, papillae
  - **Subconjunctival haemorrhage** – well-demarcated blood, no pain, no vision change; usually benign
  - **Keratitis (including HSV)** – pain, photophobia, fluorescein uptake, corneal opacity; reduced vision
  - **Anterior uveitis (iritis)** – ciliary flush, small/poorly reactive pupil, photophobia, cells/flare; normal IOP
  - **Acute angle closure glaucoma** – cloudy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, high IOP, severe pain, nausea
  - **Episcleritis/scleritis** – sectoral injection, mild pain (episcleritis) vs deep boring pain (scleritis); vision normal
  - **Orbital cellulitis** – proptosis, restricted movement, fever, RAPD
  - **Endophthalmitis** – hypopyon, vitreous haze, severe pain, recent surgery
- **Splitter:** *Fluorescein staining* — positive = keratitis > iritis; negative but photophobia + ciliary flush = iritis

### Investigations

- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart or pinhole)
  - Slit lamp examination (if available)
  - Fluorescein staining (sterile strip, cobalt blue light)
  - Intraocular pressure (tonometry or digital estimation)
  - Swab for bacterial culture if purulent discharge (for Chlamydia/gonorrhoea if sexually active)
- **Bloods:**
  - Only if suspect orbital cellulitis: FBC, CRP, blood cultures, consider ESR/ANA if uveitis recurrent
  - No routine bloods for pure red eye
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Orbital CT** (with contrast) if orbital cellulitis suspected
  - Second-line: MRI brain/orbits if cavernous sinus thrombosis
  - Not indicated for conjunctivitis, iritis, keratitis, glaucoma
- **Special tests:**
  - Corneal scrape for microbiology (if dendritic ulcer atypical or contact lens keratitis)
  - Anterior chamber paracentesis (only in endophthalmitis under ophthalmology)
- **Never-miss test:** **Fluorescein staining** – omitting this risks missing herpetic keratitis, which would be worsened by steroids

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Acute angle closure glaucoma:**
    - IV acetazolamide 500mg stat (or PO if no IV access)
    - Topical pilocarpine 2% (one drop, may repeat)
    - Topical beta-blocker (e.g. timolol 0.5% one drop)
    - Urgent ophthalmology referral
  - **Orbital cellulitis:**
    - IV ceftriaxone 2g OD + metronidazole 500mg TDS
    - CT orbit + sinuses
    - Ophthalmology + ENT referral
  - **Herpetic keratitis:**
    - Topical aciclovir 3% ointment 5 times daily
    - **NO steroids** (ever until clear by slit lamp)
    - Ophthalmology review within 24h
  - **Endophthalmitis:**
    - Urgent ophthalmology – vitreous tap + intravitreal antibiotics (do not delay in ED)
  - **Simple conjunctivitis:**
    - Chloramphenicol eye drops 0.5% – 1-2 drops q2h for 24h then q4h (first-line bacterial)
    - Fusidic acid eye drops 1% – alternative (especially if staph suspected)
    - If viral – supportive (cool compresses, artificial tears)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If admitted for orbital cellulitis: continue IV antibiotics, monitor vision, EOM, and proptosis hourly
  - If admitted for acute glaucoma (after initial drops): monitor IOP every 4h, prepare for laser iridotomy
  - If herpetic keratitis: review by ophthalmology daily; consider oral aciclovir if severe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Conjunctivitis – complete 7-day course of drops
  - Iritis – topical steroids (dexamethasone 0.1%) under ophthalmology + cycloplegic (e.g. cyclopentolate)
  - Glaucoma – long-term ophthalmology follow-up
  - Keratitis – ophthalmology follow-up for scarring
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** *Only relevant for orbital cellulitis or acute glaucoma*
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline IV at 125mL/h if septic/oral not tolerated (orbital cellulitis)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if not contraindicated)
  - **Diet:** As tolerated (unless NBM for theatre)
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 1-2h initially; local protocol for orbital cellulitis (often hourly neuro-obs including pupil checks)

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line agents:**
  - **Chloramphenicol eye drops 0.5%** – 1-2 drops q2h for 24h then q4h for 7 days (bacterial conjunctivitis)
  - **Fusidic acid eye drops 1%** – 1 drop BD for 7 days (alternative)
  - **Aciclovir eye ointment 3%** – 5 times daily for 14 days (herpetic keratitis)
  - **Acetazolamide (acute glaucoma)** – 500mg IV/PO stat
  - **Pilocarpine 2% eye drops** – 1 drop stat, may repeat in 15 min (acute glaucoma)
  - **Timolol 0.5% eye drops** – 1 drop stat (acute glaucoma)
  - **Ceftriaxone** – 2g IV OD (orbital cellulitis)
  - **Metronidazole** – 500mg IV TDS (orbital cellulitis)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Ciprofloxacin 0.3% eye drops** – for contact lens–associated keratitis (pseudomonas)
  - **Prednisolone eye drops 0.5-1%** – for iritis (only under ophthalmology)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - **Topical steroids** → absolute contraindication if herpetic keratitis suspected (until confirmed by slit lamp)
  - **Acetazolamide** → avoid in severe renal/hepatic failure, pregnancy, sulfa allergy
  - **Pilocarpine** → avoid in asthma, bradycardia
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Acetazolamide** – reduce dose in renal impairment (CrCl <50: use with caution)
  - **Ceftriaxone** – no dose adjustment needed in renal impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Topical steroids prescribed without fluorescein staining** – can cause devastating corneal damage in herpetic keratitis. **Never prescribe steroid eye drops until HSV excluded.**

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Vision loss (glaucoma, endophthalmitis, orbital cellulitis)
  - Corneal perforation (severe keratitis)
  - Cavernous sinus thrombosis (orbital cellulitis)
  - Meningitis (orbital cellulitis)
  - Steroid-induced glaucoma (if steroids used inappropriately)
- **Chronic:**
  - Corneal scarring (keratitis)
  - Synechiae (iritis)
  - Chronic glaucoma (angle closure/steroid-induced)
  - Recurrent infection (HSV keratitis)
  - Loss of vision

### Prognosis

- Simple conjunctivitis – excellent, resolves in 5-7 days with treatment.
- Acute angle closure glaucoma – guarded; each hour of IOP >40 reduces chance of vision recovery. Early treatment (<12h) has better outcome.
- Herpetic keratitis – good with prompt antivirals; recurrence common (20-50% within 1 year).
- Orbital cellulitis – good if treated early; delay can cause blindness or death.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always check visual acuity and do fluorescein staining** in every red eye – these are the two steps most likely to change your management.
- **In contact lens wearers, assume pseudomonas keratitis** until proven otherwise – refer same day to ophthalmology.
- **If you suspect iritis, put on cycloplegic (cyclopentolate 1%)** to relieve pain and prevent synechiae – but only after excluding glaucoma by IOP and slit lamp.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Prescribing topical steroids without slit lamp examination** – this is the most common serious error. It can turn herpetic keratitis into a corneal perforation.
- **Ignoring photophobia or reduced vision** – these are red flags for keratitis or iritis. Do not dismiss as "just conjunctivitis."
- **Using loperamide or antihistamines for allergic conjunctivitis without examining** – may mask underlying keratitis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**
  - Worsening pain, especially if woke from sleep
  - Vision becomes blurry or decreases
  - New photophobia or intense eye ache
  - Contact lens wearer with any new symptom
  - Fever, headache, or double vision
- **Follow-up:**
  - Conjunctivitis – GP in 3-5 days if not settling
  - Allergic – GP or optician
  - Keratitis/iritis/glaucoma – ophthalmology within 24h (or same day if sight-threatening)

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior / ophthalmology registrar** for:
  - Acute angle closure glaucoma (rock-hard eye, fixed pupil, cloudy cornea)
  - Orbital cellulitis (proptosis, fever, EOM restriction, RAPD)
  - Endophthalmitis (hypopyon, recent surgery, severe pain)
  - Herpetic keratitis (dendritic ulcer, reduced corneal sensation)
  - Any red eye with vision loss, severe pain, or suspected sight threat
- **Call ICU/CRASH team** if:
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if orbital cellulitis causing sepsis)
  - GCS <15 (cavernous sinus thrombosis)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L or signs of meningitis
